slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: Welcome to EE5907!
slides.content:
Course will be conducted by Dr. XU Yuecong (1st part, till
Recess) and Dr. Wang Si (2nd part, Wk 7 – 13).
Topics on decision theory, linear/non-linear classifier and
regressor, PCA, LDA, clustering, boosting, SVM, etc.
Syllabus available on Canvas in the Pages tab, please check
them out.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Lecture Format and Consultation
slides.content:
In-person lectures:
Lecture notes will be available right before each class.
Recording will be available at the end of each class for revision.
Break for 15 mins in between, end in 2.5 hours. After class discussion
welcomed!
Consultation hour: walk-in if you have any questions:
Mon: 14:00 – 15:30, Week 2, 5, 6. ECE Conference Room E4-05-39
Mon: 13:00 – 14:30, Week 3 (25 Aug). ECE Conference Room
Announcements will be made if there are changes for that week.
Q&A: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ph1hVnzrmUsk0-
fTsilF5OPGm2wHQQw4qrpa-pQa5Ag/edit?usp=sharing

slides.title: Background of Myself and Course
slides.content:
• Video, 3D data and time-series data analysis
• Transfer learning for efficiency and application.
• Combining with LLVM and AIOT devices.
yc.xu@nus.edu.sg
Pattern recognition and machine learning, not deep learning.
Understanding the concepts for application.
Some DL algorithms/techniques are based on PR/ML.

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: Learning Outcomes
slides.content:
Comprehend principles and core techniques in pattern
recognition such as Bayesian decision theory, linear and non-
linear classifiers, principal component analysis, linear
discriminant analysis, clustering and support vector machine.
Apply the conceptual and mathematical tools of pattern
recognition to analyze real-world data, obtaining data features
and performing classification or regression.
Assess the strengths and weaknesses of the different pattern
recognition algorithms.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: Assessment and Grading
slides.content:
Continuous Assessment consists of 3 components.
CA1: 29 Aug 2025 – 3 Oct 2025 SGT 23:59 (Week 3 – 7): 15%.
CA1 To be announced on Week 3.
Quiz: 18 Sep 2025 (During lecture on Week 6): 5%.
Quiz format: MCQ, fully open book, max 60 minutes.
Quiz to be conducted via Canvas.
CA2: TBA by Dr. Wang Si: 20%.
Final: 60%.
Closed book with one-page double-sided cheat sheet.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

Deadlines are strict. Late submission will be deducted 10 points
(out of 100) for every 24 hours. ZERO mark for late submission of
5 days (120 hours) or more.
NO plagiarism is allowed. A ZERO mark will be given to anyone
found plagiarizing and a formal report will be handed to the
Department for further investigation.
GPT-based/GPT-like tools (ChatGPT, Co-pilot, Gemini) allowed for
refinement and coding (of course, amendments are required).
They’re NOT allowed for generating the whole project submission.

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: Code of Conduct
slides.content:
Students are encouraged to actively participate.
Your grade depends on what you do, not what others do.
Don’t share your solutions/codes. Submit only your own work.
Academic Integrity:
Student’s academic work & behavior are held to highest academic integrity standards.
Cheating, plagiarism, unauthorized collaboration, and ‘helping others commit these
acts’ are examples of academic misconduct, which can result in disciplinary action.
Any violation to academic honesty will be reported to the
University's disciplinary panel.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: What is Pattern Recognition (PR)?
slides.content:
Pattern recognition is to perceive a pattern, extract the relevant info,
understand the content of the info and make decision automatically by
machine or computer.
In short: automated recognition of patterns and regularities in data.
Uses techniques developed in statistics and machine learning.
Example 1: Is the picture of an apple or pear
After perceiving the image, you may decide
left image is an apple, right image is a pear.
The decision is made with 100% certainty
(probability): how?
Based on color/shape.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: What is Pattern Recognition?
slides.content:
A more complex Example 2: Is the picture of an apple or pear
After perceiving the image, the decision is made only after extracting the
item from the correct part of the image.
If item is correctly extracted: decision can be made with 100% certainty.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

Can we always make a decision with 100% certainty?
Example 3: Is the picture of an apple or pear
Lost of certain information / features (color):
Can we still make the decision with 100% certainty? Probably not – then
how should we determine what the picture actually is?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: Pattern Recognition (PR) vs. / and Machine Learning (ML)
slides.content:
Connections or differences between PR and ML?
Machine learning: learn from data without being explicitly programmed.
Almost identical to PR, since learning implies finding patterns.
What is ML? Compare with non-learning algo (why need ML)?
Non-learning algos are bared on pre-determined rules, require human expertise. The
rules are fixed regardless the input data.
For non-learning algos, human expert should know the solution and rules – problems
that human cannot solve would NOT be solved.
Non-learning algo: human-centric; ML/PR: data-centric.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: Probability: Two Interpretations
slides.content:
Probability theory is nothing, but common sense reduced to calculation.
– Pierre Laplace, 1812
Frequentist interpretation: probabilities represent long run
frequencies of events that can happen multiple times.
Flip the coin many times, we expect it to land heads about half
the time.
Bayesian interpretation: quantify uncertainty or
ignorance about something
The coin is equally likely to land heads/tails on next toss.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

Note: for this course, probability of an event denoted as: 𝑃 𝐴 or 𝑝(𝐴).
Conditional probability of 𝐵 happening given 𝐴 has occurred:
𝑃(𝐴, 𝐵)
𝑃(𝐵|𝐴) ≜
𝑃(𝐴)
Event 𝐴 is independent of 𝐵 if 𝑃 𝐴, 𝐵 = 𝑃 𝐴 𝑃(𝐵);
Events 𝐴 ad 𝐵 are conditionally independent given 𝐶 if:
𝑃 𝐴, 𝐵 𝐶 = 𝑃 𝐴 𝐶 𝑃(𝐵|𝐶)
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: Discrete and Continuous Random Variables (RV)
slides.content:
A mathematical formalization of a quantity or object which depends
on random events.
A mapping or a function from possible outcomes in a sample space to
a measurable space, often the real numbers.
Discrete variable Continuous variable
Countable numbers of value (e.g., number of students) Infinitely many real values (e.g., time to complete a task)
Distribution defined by probability mass function (pmf) Distribution defined by cumulative distribution function
(cdf) and probability density function (pdf)
𝑃 𝑥 ≜ 𝑃(𝑋 ≤ 𝑥)
𝑑
𝑝 𝑥 ≜ 𝑃 𝑋 = 𝑥
𝑝 𝑥 ≜ 𝑃(𝑥)
𝑑𝑥
∑𝑝 𝑥 = 1
𝑏
𝑃 𝑎 < 𝑋 ≤ 𝑏 = න 𝑝 𝑥 𝑑𝑥 = 𝑃 𝑏 − 𝑃(𝑎)
𝑎
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

Discrete variable Continuous variable
Countable numbers of value (e.g., number of students) Infinitely many real values (e.g., time to complete a task)
Distribution defined by probability mass function (pmf) Distribution defined by cumulative distribution function
(cdf) and probability density function (pdf)
𝑃 𝑥 ≜ 𝑃(𝑋 ≤ 𝑥)
𝑑
𝑝 𝑥 ≜ 𝑃 𝑋 = 𝑥 𝑝 𝑥 ≜ 𝑃(𝑥)

𝑎
Important: Note whether the variable is discrete or continuous! Could be
confusing sometimes. Example:
A large quantity of green tea (e.g., 1000kg) is to be sold. 𝑋 is the weight of green tea to be
sold. Suppose any quantity can be sold, is 𝑋 discrete or continuous?
What if the green tea can only be sold in packets of 10kg, is 𝑋 now discrete or continuous?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: Bayes’ Rule
slides.content:
“Bayesian”: refer to inference methods that represent “degrees of
certainty” using probability theory, and which leverage Bayes’ rule, to
update the degree of certainty given data.
Layman term: Update our belief based on prior belief and data so that we infer
something with a certain degree of uncertainty.
Bayes’ rule: a formula for computing the probability distribution over
possible values of an unknown quantity 𝐻 given observed data 𝑌 = 𝑦.
𝑝 𝐻 = ℎ 𝑝(𝑌 = 𝑦|𝐻 = ℎ)
𝑝 𝐻 = ℎ 𝑌 = 𝑦 =
𝑝(𝑌 = 𝑦)
Follows the product rule of probability: 𝑝 ℎ 𝑦 𝑝 𝑦 = 𝑝 ℎ 𝑝 𝑦 ℎ = 𝑝(ℎ, 𝑦)
𝑝 𝐻 = ℎ 𝑌 = 𝑦 : posterior distribution / conditional distribution.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: Bayes’ Rule: in Detail
slides.content:
𝑝 𝐻 = ℎ 𝑝(𝑌 = 𝑦|𝐻 = ℎ)
𝑝 𝐻 = ℎ 𝑌 = 𝑦 =
𝑝(𝑌 = 𝑦)
𝑝(𝐻): what we know about possible values of 𝐻 before we see any data – the prior
distribution (layman terms: prior belief).
𝑝(𝑌|𝐻 = ℎ): the distribution over possible outcomes 𝑌 we expect to see if 𝐻 = ℎ – the
likelihood (layman terms: the observed data).
Multiplying the prior distribution and the likelihood – unnormalized joint distribution 𝑝(𝐻 =
ℎ, 𝑌 = 𝑦). Convert into a normalized distribution by dividing by 𝑝(𝑌 = 𝑦), which is
denoted as the marginal likelihood.
𝑝(𝐻 = ℎ|𝑌 = 𝑦): represents our new belief state (updated belief) about the possible
values of 𝐻 – the posterior distribution.
posterior ∝ prior × likelihood
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

365

𝑃 𝐻 𝑃(𝑌|𝐻) 𝑃 𝐻 𝑃(𝑌|𝐻)
𝑃 𝐻 𝑌 = =
𝑃 𝑌 𝑃(𝑌|𝐻)𝑃 𝐻 + 𝑃(𝑌|𝐻 ෩ )𝑃 𝐻 ෩
0.0137(0.9)

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: Bernoulli Distribution
slides.content:
One of the simplest probability distribution – can be used to model binary
events. (E.g., coin flipping)
Explaining the Bernoulli distribution with coin flipping. Denote 𝑌 = 1 as coin lands on
heads, and 𝑌 = 0 as coin lands on tails.
Suppose the probability of the coin lands on heads is 0 ≤ 𝜃 ≤ 1. Thus, 𝑝 𝑌 = 1 = 𝜃
and 𝑝 𝑌 = 0 = 1 − 𝜃. Then random variable 𝑌 follow the Bernoulli distribution:
𝑌~Ber(𝜃).
1 − 𝜃 𝑖𝑓 𝑦 = 0
𝑦 1−𝑦
Ber 𝑦 𝜃 = ቊ ≜ 𝜃 1 − 𝜃
𝜃 𝑖𝑓 𝑦 = 1
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: Binomial Distribution
slides.content:
Now, if we flip a coin multiple times. Then the sum of the Bernoulli random
variables will follow a Binomial distribution.
The Bernoulli distribution is a special case of the binomial distribution.
Suppose we observe a set of 𝑁 Bernoulli trials (e.g., toss a coin 𝑁 times), 𝑦 ~Ber(⋅
𝑛
|𝜃) for 𝑛 = 1: 𝑁. Define 𝑠 to be the total number of heads 𝑠 ≜
∑𝑁
𝕀(𝑦 = 1).
𝑛=1 𝑛
The distribution of 𝑠 follows the binomial distribution:

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: Recap: Bayes’ Rule
slides.content:
“Bayesian”: refer to inference methods that represent “degrees of
certainty” using probability theory, and which leverage Bayes’ rule, to
update the degree of certainty given data.
Layman term: Update our belief based on prior belief and data so that we infer
something with a certain degree of uncertainty.
Bayes’ rule: a formula for computing the probability distribution over
possible values of an unknown quantity 𝐻 given observed data 𝑌 = 𝑦.
𝑝 𝐻 = ℎ 𝑝(𝑌 = 𝑦|𝐻 = ℎ)
𝑝 𝐻 = ℎ 𝑌 = 𝑦 =
𝑝(𝑌 = 𝑦)
Follows the product rule of probability: 𝑝 ℎ 𝑦 𝑝 𝑦 = 𝑝 ℎ 𝑝 𝑦 ℎ = 𝑝(ℎ, 𝑦)
𝑝 𝐻 = ℎ 𝑌 = 𝑦 : posterior distribution / conditional distribution.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: Recap: Bernoulli Distribution
slides.content:
One of the simplest probability distribution – can be used to model binary
events. (E.g., coin flipping)
Explaining the Bernoulli distribution with coin flipping. Denote 𝑌 = 1 as coin lands on
heads, and 𝑌 = 0 as coin lands on tails.
Suppose the probability of the coin lands on heads is 0 ≤ 𝜃 ≤ 1. Thus, 𝑝 𝑌 = 1 = 𝜃
and 𝑝 𝑌 = 0 = 1 − 𝜃. Then random variable 𝑌 follow the Bernoulli distribution:
𝑌~Ber(𝜃).
1 − 𝜃 𝑖𝑓 𝑦 = 0
𝑦 1−𝑦
Ber 𝑦 𝜃 = ቊ ≜ 𝜃 1 − 𝜃
𝜃 𝑖𝑓 𝑦 = 1
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: Recap: Binomial Distribution
slides.content:
Now, if we flip a coin multiple times. Then the sum of the Bernoulli random
variables will follow a Binomial distribution.
The Bernoulli distribution is a special case of the binomial distribution.
Suppose we observe a set of 𝑁 Bernoulli trials (e.g., toss a coin 𝑁 times), 𝑦 ~Ber(⋅

|𝜃) for 𝑛 = 1: 𝑁. Define 𝑠 to be the total number of heads 𝑠 ≜

𝑛=1 𝑛

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: Predicting a Binary Variable
slides.content:
When we want to predict a binary variable 𝑦 ∈ {0,1} given input 𝑥 ∈ 𝒳, we
need to use a conditional probability distribution:
𝑝 𝑦 𝒙, 𝜽 = Ber(𝑦|𝑓 𝒙; 𝜽 ).
𝑓 𝒙; 𝜽 is a function that predicts the parameter of the distribution.
To avoid requiring 0 < 𝑓 𝒙; 𝜽 ≤ 1, let 𝑓 be an unconstrained function, we
use the following model:
𝑝 𝑦 𝒙, 𝜽 = Ber(𝑦|𝜎(𝑓 𝒙; 𝜽 )).
𝜎() is the sigmoid (logistic) function – “sigmoid” means S-shaped.
1
𝜎 𝑎 ≜
1 + 𝑒−𝑎

step function 𝕀(𝑎 > 0).
The sigmoid function can be thought of as a “soft” version of the heaviside
step function.

Problems for Bernoulli and Binomial distributions?
Can only model the probability for an event with ONLY 2 outcomes.
Generalize the Bernoulli to 𝐶 > 2 values.
Categorical distribution:
A discrete probability distribution with one parameter per class:

E.g., if 𝐶 = 3, then the classes are encoded as (1,0,0), (0,1,0), and (0,0,1).
As such, the categorical distribution is also written for 𝒚:

slides.title: Predicting a Categorical Variable with Softmax
slides.content:
When we want to predict a categorical variable 𝑦 given input 𝑥 ∈ 𝒳, we
need to use a conditional probability distribution:
𝑝 𝑦 𝒙, 𝜽 = Cat(𝑦|𝑓 𝒙; 𝜽 ).

slides.title: Something More on Softmax
slides.content:
1. Properties of Softmax:
a. softmax 𝒂 ∈ 0,1 , and
∑𝐶
softmax 𝒂 = 1. The softmax function has been
𝑐 𝑐=1 𝑐
applied to almost ALL classification tasks in deep learning thanks to these properties.
The input to the softmax 𝒂 = 𝑓 𝒙; 𝜽 are called logits.
2. The softmax function, at its extremes, acts like the argmax function.
Divide each 𝑎 (the components for each class of 𝒂) by a constant 𝑇, denoted as the
𝑐
Temperature. Then as 𝑇 → 0, we find the following:
1.0 if 𝑐 = arg max 𝑎
𝑐′
softmax 𝒂/𝑇 = ൝ 𝑐′
𝑐
0.0 otherwise
At low temperatures, the distribution puts most of its probability mass in the most
probable state (denoted as winner takes all), whereas at high temperatures, it
spreads the mass uniformly.

slides.title: Other Common Univariate Distributions
slides.content:
Dirac delta: when the 𝜎 of Gaussian → 0.
Student 𝑡-distribution: less sensitive to
outliers than the Gaussian distribution.
Laplace distribution: double-sided
exponential distribution.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

Beta distribution: a suitable model for the random
behavior of percentages and proportions
Gamma distribution: a flexible distribution for
positive real valued rv’s.

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: Estimating Probability Models: Model Fitting
slides.content:
Now suppose we know that the data would follow a certain probability model,
controlled by parameters 𝜽, but we don’t know what the model exactly is.
Key problem: how to learn these parameters 𝜽 from data?
The process of estimation 𝜃 from data 𝒟 is called model fitting, or equivalently,
“training” – the core of machine learning.
The training boils down to an optimization of the form:
෡
𝜽 = arg min ℒ(𝜽)
𝜽
෡
𝜽: a point estimate; ℒ(𝜽): loss function / objective function.
Important: how to define the loss function / objective function?
෡
Inference: quantify the uncertainty from data sample – compute 𝑝(𝑦|𝑥, 𝜽).
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
slides.title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)
slides.content:
The intuitive objective: pick the parameters that assign the highest prob.
to the training data (usually labelled) – maximum likelihood estimation:
෡
𝜽 = arg max 𝑝(𝒟|𝜽)
𝑚𝑙𝑒
𝜽
Assume: training samples are independently sampled from the same distribution – iid
assumption (independent and identical distributed).
𝑝 𝒟 𝜽 =
ς𝑁
𝑝(𝑦 |𝑥 , 𝜽) – likelihood; log likelihood takes its log ℓ 𝜽 ≜ log 𝑝(𝒟|𝜽).
𝑛=1 𝑛 𝑛
Most optimization algorithms are designed to min cost functions (argmin
instead of argmax), redefine the objective function to be the (conditional)
negative log likelihood (NLL): maximize likelihood = minimize NLL.
𝑁
NLL 𝜽 ≜ − log 𝑝 𝒟 𝜽 = − ෍ log 𝑝(𝑦 |𝑥 , 𝜽)
𝑛 𝑛
𝑛=1
෡
𝜽 = arg min ℒ(𝜽) = arg min NLL 𝜽

slides.title: MLE for Bernoulli Distribution
slides.content:
Suppose 𝑌 is a random variable representing a coin toss, where the event
𝑌 = 1 corresponds to heads and 𝑌 = 0 corresponds to tails.
Let 𝜃 = 𝑝(𝑌 = 1) be the probability of heads. The probability distribution for
this rv is the Bernoulli. The NLL for the Bernoulli distribution is given by:
Observe: this is just the empirical fraction of heads, an intuitive result!

If we are solving a classification problem, an intuitive loss selection would
be the 0-1 loss: (𝑓 being some kind of predictor)
